title: Grass on Polyester
timeEstimate: 15–40 minutes; chlorophyll plus protein, polyester locks both under heat
stainChemistry: Grass on polyester is two stains stuck together: green chlorophyll pigment plus plant protein. Polyester is heat-sensitive — hot water sets the protein and locks the green into the fiber permanently, plus polyester yellows over time when heat is applied to oily / pigmented residue. Polyester is also OLEOPHILIC, meaning it grabs the waxy plant lipids in grass and holds them tight. Athletic-wear polyester adds a complication: fabric softener residue from prior washes coats the fiber and blocks any cleaner from reaching the stain. The recurring trap is reaching for hot water on what looks like a 'simple' green stain — that's exactly what makes it permanent.
whyThisWorks: Cool water plus Dawn handles the protein side without setting it. Isopropyl alcohol on a cloth pulls out the chlorophyll — you can literally see the green transfer onto the cloth. Cool oxygen-bleach soak breaks down residual color. AIR-DRY is the close-out — hot dryer at any prior step locks residual chlorophyll into polyester forever and yellows the fabric. The honest cap: hot-dried grass on polyester or aged stains often plateau; pro is the right call.
defaultAssumption: Kid soccer / yard work / athletic gear on polyester or polyester-blend. Fresh = high-confidence DIY. Hot-dried = often permanent. Athletic wear with prior fabric softener buildup = strip the softener first or expect plateau.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Damp white cloth with COOL water. Press, lift, rotate. Cool only — heat sets protein and locks chlorophyll on polyester.
Generous Dawn directly on the spot. Soft toothbrush, work 5–10 min. Polyester is oleophilic — extended dwell needed for the waxy lipids in grass. Cool rinse.
Hidden test: dab 91% IPA on an inside seam, blot with white cloth. If color transfers, skip alcohol. Light or white polyester is usually fine; deeply dyed performance polyester sometimes isn't.

spottingProtocol.equipment: 91% IPA on white cotton cloth, ventilation
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply 91% IPA to a clean white cloth — never pour on the garment. Press the green spot, flip cloth as it picks up green. Outside-in. 2–3 passes. Open windows; flammable, no flame.

spottingProtocol.agent: OxiClean Cool Soak Plus Cool Wash
spottingProtocol.equipment: OxiClean per label, COOL water, washing machine
spottingProtocol.instruction: OxiClean cool soak 30 min. Cool machine wash with normal detergent. NO fabric softener (coats fiber, blocks future cleaning). NEVER hot water — sets stain on polyester permanently.
AIR-DRY only. Inspect in daylight. Faint green = repeat alcohol pass. NEVER hot dryer until clear — heat locks chlorophyll into polyester permanently and yellows fabric over time.

steps: 2. Generous Dawn pretreat, cool, 5–10 min, soft toothbrush.
3. Hidden-area IPA test on dyed polyester.
4. 91% IPA cloth tamp for chlorophyll, outside-in, 2–3 passes.
5. OxiClean cool soak 30 min plus cool machine wash.
6. AIR-DRY plus daylight inspect — never heat until clear.
products: 91% Isopropyl alcohol (hidden test on dyed polyester)
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool throughout — heat sets protein and locks chlorophyll on polyester
warnings: NEVER hot water — sets protein, locks chlorophyll into polyester
NEVER hot dryer until clear — locks chlorophyll, yellows polyester
NEVER chlorine bleach on polyester — damages fiber
NEVER fabric softener on athletic wear — coats fiber, blocks cleaning
Hidden test on dyed polyester before alcohol
Polyester is oleophilic — Dawn pretreat is the unlock
Flammable solvent — no flame near alcohol

homeSolutions.agent: Two Stains Stuck Together — Pigment Plus Protein
homeSolutions.instruction: Grass is the green pigment plus plant protein. Each part needs different treatment: cool Dawn handles the protein, rubbing alcohol pulls out the chlorophyll. Doing only one half is why grass stains come back after the wash. Two hard rules on polyester: NO hot water (locks the green) and NO hot dryer until cleared (locks chlorophyll permanently and yellows the fabric).
Damp white cloth with COOL water. Press, lift, rotate. Cool only. Polyester punishes any heat at any step.

Polyester is oleophilic — it grabs the waxy plant lipids in grass tight. Apply a generous amount of Dawn directly to the spot. Work in with a soft toothbrush for 5 to 10 minutes. Cool rinse. Athletic-wear polyester sometimes has fabric softener buildup that blocks cleaning — if past treatments plateau on the same spot, that's the cause.

Before alcohol on dyed performance polyester: dab a drop of 91% isopropyl alcohol on an inside seam, blot with a white cloth. If color transfers, skip alcohol. Light or white polyester is usually fine; deeply dyed athletic wear sometimes loses color.

homeSolutions.agent: Isopropyl Alcohol on a Cloth — Pulls the Green Out
homeSolutions.instruction: This is the chlorophyll step. Apply 91% isopropyl alcohol to a clean white cloth — never pour on the garment. Press the green spot. Flip the cloth as it picks up green. Outside-in. You'll see the chlorophyll transfer onto the cloth. 2 to 3 passes. Open windows — alcohol is flammable, no flame.

homeSolutions.agent: OxiClean Cool Soak Plus Cool Wash — No Softener
homeSolutions.instruction: OxiClean per label in COOL water, soak 30 minutes. Then cool machine wash with normal detergent. SKIP fabric softener this wash — it coats the fiber and blocks any future cleaning. NEVER hot water at any step.

Hang to AIR-DRY. Inspect in daylight before the dryer. Faint green = repeat the alcohol pass before any heat. Hot dryer on residual chlorophyll = permanent on polyester plus yellowing of the fabric over time.

steps: 1. NO hot water. NO hot dryer until clear.
3. Generous Dawn pretreat, cool, 5–10 min, soft toothbrush.
4. Hidden-area IPA test on dyed polyester.
5. 91% IPA on cloth, tamp green outside-in, 2–3 passes.
6. OxiClean cool soak 30 min plus cool wash, no fabric softener.
7. AIR-DRY plus daylight inspect — no dryer until fully clear.
products: 91% Isopropyl alcohol (hidden test on dyed polyester)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if hot water or hot dryer was already used (often permanent + yellowing on polyester). Stop if hidden-seam test shows color loss. Stop on performance / specialty / dry-clean-only polyester (pro). Stop after 2 home rounds without progress.

escalation.when: Hot water or hot dryer already used (often permanent + yellowing). Performance / specialty / dry-clean-only polyester. Hidden-area test shows color loss. 2 home rounds plateau. Old grass through multiple wash cycles.
escalation.whatToTell: "Grass on [white / dyed / athletic / blend] polyester, age [fresh / days / hot-dried suspected], home-treated with [Dawn cool pretreat + IPA cloth tamp + OxiClean cool soak + cool wash + air-dry]. No hot, no chlorine, no softener. [Residual green / yellow shadow / hot-dried suspected]."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner with polyester / athletic-wear experience

contextQuestions: Athletic wear, white tee, dyed performance fabric, or blend?
Fresh, days old, or hot-dried already?
Has fabric softener been used in past washes (athletic gear)?
Visible green or just yellow shadow remaining?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Polyester is oleophilic AND heat-sensitive — both push toward strict cool discipline plus extended Dawn dwell. Athletic-wear fabric softener buildup is the hidden plateau cause. Hot-dried grass on polyester is often permanent because heat both sets the protein and locks the chlorophyll into the fiber. Performance polyester (compression, technical, Gore-Tex) goes to pro because the fabric coatings react unpredictably to alcohol and oxygen bleach.
